title: Mastering the Digital SAT: What Changes for You?
author: Tklesson Team
content:
The SAT has gone digital. If you're used to paper practice tests, the new format can feel disorienting. But digital also offers tools that paper never could. Here's how to adapt and succeed.

The Big Changes

Shorter test: The digital SAT is about 2 hours and 14 minutes, down from 3 hours on paper. Fewer questions means each question matters more.

Adaptive testing: The test adapts to your performance. The first module of each section has a mix of easy, medium, and hard questions. Your performance determines whether the second module is easier or harder. Doing well on module 1 gives you a harder module 2 — which is how you earn a high score.

Reading passages are shorter: Instead of long passages with many questions, each passage is shorter (about 50-100 words) and paired with one question. This is good news if you struggle with long reading sections.

Built-in calculator: A graphing calculator is embedded in the test app. You can use it for the entire math section. No more worrying about bringing your own or running out of batteries.

The New Tools You Need to Practice

Flagging questions: You can flag any question and come back to it later. This is essential for time management. Skip hard questions, flag them, and return at the end.

Eliminating answer choices: You can cross out answers you know are wrong. This reduces visual clutter and helps you focus on remaining options.

Zoom feature: If reading on screen is hard, zoom in. The test interface allows you to enlarge text and images.

Reference sheet: Math formulas are available in the app. No need to memorize area formulas or trigonometric identities.

Practice using these tools before test day. Don't learn them during the real exam.

Reading Section Strategies (Digital)

Read the question first. With shorter passages, you can read the question, then scan for the answer. This is faster than reading the passage cold.

Use the highlighter. The digital test has a highlighter tool. Use it to mark evidence for your answer. This helps you stay focused and verify your choice.

Beware of 'close but not quite' answers. Digital SAT reading questions often have two answers that seem correct. One will have a subtle flaw. Read every word of each option.

Don't spend more than 90 seconds per question. Flag and move on. You can come back if you have time.

Math Section Strategies (Digital)

Use the calculator strategically. The embedded calculator is powerful but slower than mental math for simple operations. Don't use it for 2+3. Save it for complex calculations.

Desmos graphing is your secret weapon. The digital SAT includes a Desmos graphing calculator. You can graph equations and find intersection points visually. This is often faster than solving algebraically.

Show your work on scratch paper. Even though the test is digital, you'll have scratch paper. Write down each step. Careless errors are the #1 reason for wrong answers.

Check for 'trap answers.' The SAT loves to include answers that result from common mistakes. If your answer appears immediately without any work, it might be a trap.

Practice with Bluebook

The College Board has a free app called Bluebook. It contains official digital practice tests that exactly simulate the real exam. Take at least 3-4 practice tests before your real SAT. Familiarity with the interface is almost as important as content knowledge.

Review your mistakes after each practice test. Don't just look at the right answer. Understand why you picked the wrong one and how to avoid that error next time.
